---
title: "Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance"
lang: en-GB
---

## Introduction {#sec-11-intro .unnumbered}

Clinical information systems handle the most sensitive category of data that
exists: health data. Its circulation raises questions that go beyond engineering
— *under what right*, *under what conditions*, and *with what guarantees for
the patient*?

The answer to these questions rests on two complementary legal instruments:
the General Data Protection Regulation [@RGPD2016] and Decree-Law No. 125/2025
[@DL125_2025], which transposes the NIS2 Directive [@NIS2Dir2022] into
Portuguese law. Beyond the legal framework, this chapter covers the
anonymisation mechanisms that make it possible to use clinical data for
research, the principles of *Privacy by Design*, and the institutional
governance that articulates the obligations of both regimes.

## Legal Framework {#sec-11-legal}

### General Data Protection Regulation {#sec-11-rgpd}

The GDPR [@RGPD2016] defines in Article 4 the concept of *personal data* as
"any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person".
Health data are treated as a *special category* (Article 9), subject to a more
restrictive regime: their processing is prohibited by default, only being
permitted on an explicit legal basis — the data subject's consent, vital
interests, health care, scientific research with adequate safeguards, or
another basis recognised by European or national law.

The GDPR distinguishes two fundamental roles in the chain of responsibility:

| Role | Definition (Art. 4) | Hospital example |
|:-----|:--------------------|:----------------|
| **Data Controller** | The entity that determines the purposes and means of processing | The hospital |
| **Data Processor** | The entity that processes data on behalf of the controller | PACS vendor, cloud provider |

: Roles of responsibility under the GDPR [@RGPD2016]. {#tbl-rgpd-papeis}

Legal responsibility rests with the *data controller* even when the actual
processing is carried out by a data processor. The contract between them — the
Data Processing Agreement (DPA) — is mandatory and must specify the purposes,
the categories of data processed, the required security measures, and the
conditions for data deletion (Article 28 GDPR).

The **DPO** (*Data Protection Officer*, Article 37) is mandatory for entities
that process health data on a large scale, notably hospitals. The DPO monitors
compliance, advises the controller, and serves as a point of contact with the
national supervisory authority — the CNPD (*Comissão Nacional de Protecção de
Dados*, Portugal's Data Protection Authority). Articles 30 (records of
processing activities) and 32 (technical security measures) have direct
architectural implications for clinical systems: every access to clinical data
must be logged and every connection between systems must be encrypted.

### Decree-Law No. 125/2025 — NIS2 Regime {#sec-11-nis2}

In force since 3 April 2026, Decree-Law No. 125/2025 [@DL125_2025] transposes
the NIS2 Directive [@NIS2Dir2022] into Portuguese law, revoking Law No.
46/2018. This legislation creates a second legal regime running in parallel with
the GDPR, focused exclusively on the security of networks and information
systems.

DL 125/2025 classifies covered entities based on the sectors listed in Annexes
I and II:

| Classification | Sector | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Essential entity** (Annex I) | Sector 5 — Health | Hospitals; healthcare providers |
| **Important entity** (Annex II) | Sector 7 — Research | Research organisations; universities |

: Classification of hospital and academic entities under DL 125/2025 [@DL125_2025]. {#tbl-nis2-classif}

The distinction has direct implications for fines: for essential entities, the
most serious infringements can reach € 10,000,000 or 2% of annual worldwide
turnover; for important entities, the limit is € 7,000,000 or 1.4% (Article 61).

The main obligations introduced by DL 125/2025 include:

- **Cybersecurity measures** (Article 27): risk management policies,
  operational continuity, supply chain security, cryptography, multi-factor
  authentication, and training.
- **Cybersecurity Officer** (Article 31): mandatory designation, distinct from
  the DPO — the DPO answers to the GDPR and the CNPD; the Cybersecurity Officer
  answers to NIS2 and the CNCS (*Centro Nacional de Cibersegurança*, Portugal's
  National Cybersecurity Centre).
- **Registration with CNCS** (Article 35): within 60 days of qualification.
- **Annual cybersecurity report** (Article 30): submitted to CNCS by the last
  working day of January.
- **Incident notification** (Articles 40–44): initial alert to CNCS within
  24 hours, update within 72 hours, final report within 30 working days.

Article 79 establishes a coordination mechanism between the CNCS and the CNPD:
when an infringement of cybersecurity obligations simultaneously constitutes a
personal data breach, the CNCS notifies the CNPD. The two regimes are
complementary — the same audit architecture serves both.

## Anonymisation and the Risk of Re-identification {#sec-11-anonimizacao}

### Anonymisation, Pseudonymisation and Encryption {#sec-11-anon-distinc}

The GDPR distinguishes three concepts that are frequently confused in practice:

**Anonymisation** is the irreversible transformation that prevents the
identification of the data subject. Recital 26 of the GDPR — one of the
introductory paragraphs that guide the interpretation of the articles —
establishes that genuinely anonymised data are not personal data and fall
outside the scope of the regulation. The criterion is demanding: it is not
sufficient that the controller cannot reverse the process — it is necessary
that no third party, using reasonably available means, could do so either.

**Pseudonymisation** (Article 4(5)) is the replacement of direct identifiers
with a pseudonym, whilst retaining a correspondence key stored separately.
Pseudonymised data *remain personal data*: anyone with access to the key can
re-identify the data subject. Pseudonymisation is recognised by the GDPR as a
security measure that reduces risk (Article 32), but does not eliminate legal
obligations.

**Encryption** transforms data into an unreadable format without the decryption
key. It is not anonymisation: encrypted data remain personal data — anyone with
the key can recover them. Encryption is a mandatory security measure for data in
transit and strongly recommended for data at rest.

| Technique | Personal data? | GDPR applies? |
|:----------|:--------------|:-------------|
| Identifiable data | Yes | Yes |
| Pseudonymised data | Yes | Yes |
| Encrypted data | Yes | Yes |
| Anonymised data | No | No (Recital 26) |

: Scope of the GDPR according to data type [@RGPD2016]. {#tbl-anon-rgpd}

### The Risk of Re-identification {#sec-11-reident}

The principal reason why genuine anonymisation is difficult to guarantee is the
risk of re-identification through *attribute linkage*. Even without a name or
identification number, the combination of apparently innocuous attributes can
uniquely single out an individual. Three real-world cases illustrate this risk.

**The Sweeney study (2000)** is the founding reference for this phenomenon.
Latanya Sweeney analysed health data from the state of Massachusetts, published
as "anonymised" with names and addresses removed, and found that 87% of
Americans could be uniquely identified using only three attributes: postal code,
date of birth, and sex [@Sweeney2000]. The demonstration was unequivocal:
Sweeney cross-referenced the health file with publicly registered voter rolls
and identified the state governor from his postal code, date of birth, and sex —
information present in the "anonymised" data.

**The Netflix case (2006–2008)** extended this vulnerability to behavioural
data. In 2006, Netflix published 100 million film ratings from 480,000 users,
replacing names with numerical identifiers, for a competition to improve its
recommendation algorithm. In 2008, Narayanan and Shmatikov demonstrated that
these data were vulnerable to re-identification [@NarayananShmatikov2008]. The
method involved cross-referencing the anonymous ratings with public ratings from
the same user on IMDb: just 8 ratings with approximate dates (a margin of two
days) were sufficient to identify a user with a probability greater than 99%.
Netflix was taken to court and cancelled the second Netflix Prize competition
(for film recommendations). The study demonstrated that the re-identification of
sparse datasets is computationally feasible even without access to the
pseudonymisation key.

**The AOL Search case (2006)** illustrated the same vulnerability in textual
data. In August 2006, AOL released 20 million searches from 658,000 users over
three months, with names replaced by numerical IDs. Days later, the *New York
Times* identified user no. 4417749 through the content of her searches: chronic
diseases, family members' names, location, and personal preferences
[@BarbaroZeller2006]. The user, a 62-year-old woman from Georgia, had not made
a single search with her own name — but the totality of her searches was, in
practice, a unique fingerprint. AOL dismissed those responsible for the release
and the case prompted specific legislation in the United States.

In a clinical context, the risk is amplified. Health data have far more
attributes than behavioural data: diagnoses (including rare diseases), admission
dates, attending physician, medication combinations, laboratory results, and
medical images. A rare disease can, in itself, be identifying in a given
geographical area.

### k-Anonymity {#sec-11-k-anonimato}

*k-Anonymity* is the most widely used formal model for quantifying and
controlling the risk of re-identification through attribute linkage. It was
defined by Sweeney in 2002 [@Sweeney2002]:

> A dataset satisfies *k-anonymity* if, for every combination of values of the
> *quasi-identifier* attributes, there are at least *k* records with that same
> combination — making each individual indistinguishable from at least *k* − 1
> others.

**Quasi-identifier attributes** are those that, individually, do not identify
the data subject, but that combined can single them out. In the clinical context,
typical quasi-identifiers are: postal code, date of birth, sex, ethnicity, and
type of admission. Directly identifying attributes (name, patient number) are
usually suppressed in a first step — k-anonymity handles those that remain.

To illustrate, consider a hospital table with 8 records:

| Postal code | Age | Sex | Diagnosis |
|:-----------|:----|:----|:---------|
| 5000-xxx | 23 | M | Pneumonia |
| 5000-xxx | 23 | M | Bronchitis |
| 4200-xxx | 45 | F | Diabetes |
| 4200-xxx | 45 | F | Hypertension |
| 4200-xxx | 45 | F | Renal disease |
| 3800-xxx | 67 | M | Oncology |
| 3800-xxx | 67 | M | Cardiac disease |
| 1600-xxx | 31 | F | Arthritis |

: Example of a hospital table without k-anonymity. {#tbl-kanon-original}

The record `1600-xxx / 31 / F` is unique (k = 1): anyone who knows this
patient's postal code, age, and sex can immediately identify them and learn
their diagnosis. To achieve 2-anonymity, it would be necessary to generalise
the quasi-identifiers of the problematic records — for example, broadening the
postal code to the first two digits or generalising the age to an age band.
This tension between *utility* (the value of data for research) and *privacy*
(protection of the data subject) is the central problem of anonymisation.

**Limitations of k-anonymity.** k-Anonymity protects against linkage attacks,
but not against *homogeneity attacks*: if within a group of *k* records all
values of the sensitive attribute (the diagnosis) are identical, an attacker
who knows that their target belongs to the group learns the diagnosis without
needing to identify the individual record. To address this problem, Machanavajjhala
and colleagues proposed *l-diversity* in 2007 [@Machanavajjhala2007]: a dataset
satisfies *l-diversity* if, within each quasi-identifier group, the sensitive
attribute takes at least *l* well-represented values — preventing the group from
being homogeneous with respect to sensitive information.

### Anonymisation Techniques {#sec-11-tecnicas}

The data privacy literature [@ElEmamArbuckle2013; @ENISA2019pseudo] identifies
six main techniques, each with different characteristics and trade-offs:

**Suppression** is the complete removal of an attribute or record. It is
typically applied to direct identifiers (name, patient record number, address)
before any other processing.

**Generalisation** replaces a specific value with a less precise value belonging
to a broader category. It is the central mechanism of k-anonymity: an exact
postal code becomes a region; an exact date of birth becomes an age band; a rare
diagnosis is mapped to its more general nosological category.

**Perturbation** (or noise addition) introduces controlled statistical
variations: dates are shifted by a random number of days; numerical values
receive a random increment within a defined interval. Perturbation belongs to
the family of *differential privacy*, which formally guarantees that the
presence or absence of a specific individual has negligible impact on published
statistics.

**Pseudonymisation** replaces direct identifiers with synthetic identifiers,
retaining a correspondence table stored separately with strict access controls.
The data remain personal data, but the technique is useful in longitudinal
studies where it is necessary to track the same individual over time without
exposing their identity to all participants.

**Synthesis** produces a new artificial dataset with the same statistical
properties as the original, without any direct correspondence to real records.
Techniques such as Generative Adversarial Networks (*GANs*) are used to generate
synthetic clinical data for training machine learning models, eliminating the
source risk.

**Aggregation** publishes only statistics computed over groups of records —
means, distributions, counts — without exposing individual data. It is the
technique with the lowest residual re-identification risk, but also the one that
imposes the greatest loss of utility for detailed research.

| Technique | Reversible? | Utility loss | Typical use case |
|:----------|:-----------|:------------|:----------------|
| Suppression | No | High | Direct identifiers |
| Generalisation | No | Medium | k-anonymity in research |
| Perturbation | No | Low–Medium | Model training |
| Pseudonymisation | Yes (with key) | Low | Longitudinal studies |
| Synthesis | N/A | Variable | Clinical AI training |
| Aggregation | No | Very high | Public health reports |

: Comparison of anonymisation techniques [@ElEmamArbuckle2013; @ENISA2019pseudo]. {#tbl-tecnicas-anon}

### Health Data — Why the Sensitivity is Different {#sec-11-sensibilidade}

Health data combine three properties that make them especially sensitive:

**Diagnostic specificity** stems from the rarity of certain conditions. Rare
diseases have, by definition, few cases per geographical area — which makes the
diagnosis itself identifying at a local level. The combination of three common
diagnoses can be equally identifying in a given hospital.

**Temporal persistence** is a unique property of health data. A diagnosis from
1995 may still be sensitive in 2025 — or in 2045. Financial data expire,
location data change, but health data remain relevant throughout the data
subject's lifetime and, in the case of genomic data, beyond it.

**Social impact potential** manifests as discrimination in employment (chronic,
oncological, and mental health conditions) and exclusion from insurance products.
In the case of genomic data, identification also compromises the privacy of
biological relatives who never participated in the treatment: Gymrek and
colleagues demonstrated in 2013 that DNA sequences from men can be linked to
the family surname with high precision using publicly available genealogical
databases [@Gymrek2013]; Erlich and Narayanan established in 2014 that an
individual can be genetically identified even within anonymised datasets,
compromising the privacy of their relatives [@ErlichNarayanan2014].

## Technical Privacy Standards in Clinical Environments {#sec-11-normas}

The legal framework is realised through four technical instruments used together
by clinical systems:

**DICOM file anonymisation.** A DICOM file is not just an image — it is a
container with hundreds of structured attributes that include the patient's
name, date of birth, referring physician, and institution name. The DICOM
standard defines in Annex E of Part 3.15 a set of *Confidentiality Profiles*
that specify, for each attribute, which action to apply (deletion, replacement
with an empty value, or generation of a new identifier). There is also a
specific risk — *burned-in text* — where the patient's name is recorded
directly onto the image pixels and escapes attribute-level processing.

**FHIR Consent.** The FHIR R4 `Consent` resource enables the patient's consent
to be represented in a *machine-readable* form — directly readable by the
server, without human interpretation. The server automatically verifies, before
serving any clinical resource, whether the requested access is covered by an
active and valid consent, for which purposes and for how long.

**SMART on FHIR.** SMART on FHIR (*Substitutable Medical Apps, Reusable
Technologies on FHIR*) defines how an application obtains authorisation to
access FHIR data on behalf of a patient or healthcare professional
[@MandelSMART2016; @HL7_SMART2023]. It is built on the OAuth 2.0 protocol and
adds granular authorisations by FHIR resource type and clinical purpose.

**ATNA — Audit Trail as a Dual Legal Obligation.** The IHE ATNA (*Audit Trail
and Node Authentication*) profile [@IHE_ATNA2024] logs every access to clinical
data in a persistent audit trail. With DL 125/2025 in force, ATNA serves two
regimes simultaneously: it implements the records of processing activities
required by GDPR Article 30 and the security measures of Article 32; and it
provides the documentary evidence needed to notify an incident to CNCS within
the 24-hour deadline imposed by Article 40 of DL 125/2025. A hospital without
an audit log may not have the capacity to reconstruct the timeline of an
incident — compromising the mandatory notification.

## *Privacy by Design* {#sec-11-pbd}

The concept of *Privacy by Design* was formulated by Ann Cavoukian, Privacy
Commissioner of Ontario, in the early 1990s, and systematised into seven
principles [@Cavoukian2011]. Article 25 of the GDPR converted these principles
from best practice into a legal obligation.

**1. Proactive, not reactive.** Privacy is designed before problems occur. The
privacy requirements — what data are needed, for how long, with what access
controls — are defined before choosing the architecture, not after the system
is in production.

**2. Privacy as the default setting.** The maximum level of privacy is the
default state of the system. The patient need not do anything to protect their
data — it is the system that must be explicitly configured to share.

**3. Privacy embedded into design.** Privacy is not a layer added at the end of
development, but an integral part of the architecture. Access control verified
directly by the clinical server is an example of embedded privacy; an external
module that checks consent in a separate layer is the opposite pattern.

**4. Full functionality.** Privacy and functionality are not mutually exclusive.
The data needed for research can be obtained with adequate anonymisation without
compromising scientific utility; granular consent allows specific purposes to be
authorised without blocking the entire system.

**5. End-to-end security.** Protection applies throughout the entire data
lifecycle: from creation to archiving and deletion. Encryption in transit and
at rest, and verified and documented secure deletion processes are the technical
expressions of this principle.

**6. Visibility and transparency.** The patient knows what is done with their
data. Audit logs must be accessible to the patient on request; data breaches
must be notified in plain language (GDPR Article 34).

**7. Respect for user privacy.** The system is centred on the data subject's
needs. Granular consent, the right of access, rectification and erasure (GDPR
Article 17), and an interface that allows the patient to manage their
preferences are implementations of this principle.

## Health Data Governance {#sec-11-governanca}

### Roles and Responsibilities {#sec-11-gov-papeis}

Health data governance articulates roles defined by the GDPR with the new roles
introduced by DL 125/2025:

| Role | Regime | Responsibility | External interlocutor |
|:-----|:-------|:--------------|:---------------------|
| **Data Controller** | GDPR Art. 4 | Defines purposes and means | CNPD |
| **Data Processor** | GDPR Art. 4 | Processes data on behalf of controller | Controller (via DPA) |
| **DPO** | GDPR Art. 37 | Monitors GDPR compliance | CNPD |
| **Cybersecurity Officer** | DL 125/2025 Art. 31 | Proposes NIS2 measures; signs annual report | CNCS |
| **Point of contact** | DL 125/2025 Art. 32 | 24/7 availability for incidents | CNCS / CERT.PT |

: Governance roles — GDPR and DL 125/2025 [@RGPD2016; @DL125_2025]. {#tbl-gov-papeis}

The DPO answers to the CNPD within the scope of personal data protection; the
Cybersecurity Officer answers to the CNCS within the scope of network and
information security. A hospital must have both figures with non-overlapping
functions — although they must collaborate when a cybersecurity incident
simultaneously involves a personal data breach (Article 79 of DL 125/2025).

### Competent Authorities {#sec-11-gov-autoridades}

**CNPD** (*Comissão Nacional de Protecção de Dados*) is the national supervisory
authority for the GDPR in Portugal. It may impose fines of up to € 20,000,000
or 4% of annual worldwide turnover. It receives notifications of personal data
breaches within 72 hours (GDPR Article 33).

**CNCS** (*Centro Nacional de Cibersegurança*) is the competent authority for
supervising DL 125/2025 in Portugal. It manages the register of essential and
important entities, receives annual cybersecurity reports, and is the recipient
of incident notifications. **CERT.PT** is integrated within the CNCS as the
operational incident response team.

**ENISA** (*European Union Agency for Cybersecurity*) is the European reference
agency for cybersecurity and publishes technical guidance [@ENISA2019pseudo],
coordinating the network of national CSIRTs of which CERT.PT is a member.

**EDPB** (*European Data Protection Board*) issues harmonised guidelines for
the 27 Member States, binding on national authorities such as the CNPD.

### NIS2 Obligations for Hospitals and Universities {#sec-11-gov-nis2-obrig}

| Obligation | Article | Deadline |
|:-----------|:--------|:--------|
| Registration with CNCS | Art. 35 | 60 days after qualification |
| Designation of Cybersecurity Officer | Art. 31 | 20 working days |
| Annual cybersecurity report | Art. 30 | Last working day of January |
| Initial incident alert | Art. 40 | 24 hours |
| Incident update | Art. 41 | 72 hours |
| Final incident report | Art. 43 | 30 working days |

: NIS2 obligations and respective deadlines for hospitals (essential entities) [@DL125_2025]. {#tbl-nis2-obrigacoes}

## Summary {#sec-11-sintese .unnumbered}

| Concept | Key point |
|:--------|:---------|
| **Health data** | Special category (GDPR Art. 9) — processing prohibited by default |
| **Anonymisation** | Irreversible; outside the scope of the GDPR (Recital 26) |
| **Pseudonymisation** | Personal data — reduces risk but maintains GDPR obligations |
| **k-anonymity** | Each record indistinguishable from at least k−1 others on quasi-identifiers |
| **l-diversity** | k-anonymity + diversity of sensitive values within each group |
| **ATNA** | Access audit = GDPR compliance (Art. 30+32) and NIS2 (Art. 27+40) |
| **DPO** | Mandatory in hospitals — answers to GDPR before the CNPD |
| **Cybersecurity Officer** | Mandatory under DL 125/2025 — distinct from DPO; answers to CNCS |
| **Hospitals** | Essential entities (Annex I, Sector 5 — DL 125/2025) |
| **Universities** | Important entities (Annex II, Sector 7 — DL 125/2025) |
| ***Privacy by Design*** | GDPR Art. 25 — privacy from conception, not added at the end |

: Summary of Chapter 11 concepts. {#tbl-sintese-11}

The medical informatics engineer has direct responsibility in three domains:

- **By law (GDPR + NIS2):** health data processed only on an explicit legal
  basis; audit logs maintained as evidence of compliance; encryption on all
  connections; DPA contract with all data processors; Cybersecurity Officer
  designated and registered with CNCS; incident notifications to CNCS within
  the legal deadlines.
- **By architecture:** data anonymised before leaving the hospital for research
  purposes; consent verified by the server before serving any clinical resource;
  audit of all accesses to clinical data.
- **By process:** DPIA (*Data Protection Impact Assessment*) before new systems
  processing health data; DPO involved in architecture decisions; notification
  of breaches to the CNPD within 72 hours (GDPR Article 33); training for all
  staff with access to clinical data.

## Suggested Exercises {#sec-11-exercicios .unnumbered}

**1.** A hospital research department wishes to share clinical records with a
university for training a sepsis detection model.

(a) Which GDPR article establishes that health data are a special category and
    what type of legal basis is required for their processing?
(b) The team removes only the patient's name and patient number from the records.
    Argue technically why these data may not be genuinely anonymised, using the
    concept of quasi-identifier.
(c) Propose an anonymisation process that reduces the re-identification risk to
    a reasonable level, specifying the techniques used and the attributes to which
    each is applied.
(d) After anonymisation, are the data subject to the GDPR? Justify with reference
    to the regulation's articles.

**2.** A hospital has a database of 10,000 patients with the following attributes:
postal code (4 digits), age band (5 years), sex, and primary diagnosis.

(a) Formally define the concept of k-anonymity and explain what it means to say
    that this database satisfies 5-anonymity.
(b) A quasi-identifier group has 7 records, all with the diagnosis "breast
    neoplasm". The table satisfies 7-anonymity. What attack allows the diagnosis
    of this group to be revealed without identifying the individual record?
(c) What formal property was proposed by Machanavajjhala and colleagues to
    mitigate this attack? Describe informally what it consists of.
(d) The hospital applies generalisation and increases k from 5 to 10. What effect
    does this operation have on the statistical utility of the data for research?

**3.** A hospital suffers an unauthorised access to its medical imaging archive
system during the night. The intruder accessed files belonging to 340 patients
that had not been anonymised.

(a) Under the GDPR, which article obliges the notification of this breach to the
    CNPD? What is the deadline?
(b) Must the CNCS also be notified? Under which legislation and which article?
    Within what deadline?
(c) The hospital had no access audit system implemented. What specific operational
    difficulties does this absence create in responding to the incident and in
    meeting the mandatory notification requirements?
(d) The DPO and the Cybersecurity Officer have distinct roles in this incident.
    Describe the specific responsibility of each.

**4.** Consider the three re-identification cases studied in this chapter
(Sweeney, Netflix, AOL).

(a) Which attributes were used as quasi-identifiers in each case? Build a
    comparative table.
(b) All three cases involved data published as "anonymised". Would Recital 26
    of the GDPR protect the data subjects in any of the cases? Justify.
(c) Which anonymisation technique could have reduced the re-identification risk
    in the Netflix case? And in the AOL case?
(d) Genomic data present a different re-identification risk from behavioural data.
    Explain the difference, with reference to the research by Gymrek et al. (2013)
    and Erlich & Narayanan (2014).
